definition: Financial repression is the most likely fiscal resolution path for aging advanced economies (~50-60% probability), but its effectiveness at deleveraging is demographically contingent. Repression with demographic tailwinds (US 1946-1980, labor force growth ~1.5%) produced successful deleveraging; without tailwinds produces Japan-style stasis.
evidence: Historical base rate: 3/3 own-currency advanced economies with high debt + aging chose repression
US 1946-1980, UK 1945-1980, Japan 1990-present all followed repression path
Aging political economy ratchet has ~1/20 counterexample rate (only Germany's Schuldenbremse)
US at 0.3% labor force growth structurally closer to Japan pattern than 1946-1980 pattern
France 2023 pension reform confirmed the political mechanism
open_questions: Can AI-driven productivity substitute for the missing demographic growth tailwind?
How long can prolonged moderate negative real returns persist without triggering capital flight?
Does the US reserve currency status change the repression dynamics?
